2024-02-28 7 min read by Matthew Brunken

Training Through Nebraska's Four Seasons

From hot summer mornings to cold winter trails, learn how to adapt your running routine to Nebraska's diverse weather conditions.

Nebraska is known for its dramatic seasonal changes, and as a runner based between Lincoln and Omaha, I've learned to embrace and adapt to all of them. Here's how to make the most of running through Nebraska's four distinct seasons.

Spring: The Rebirth Season

Spring in Nebraska can be unpredictable, with temperatures ranging from chilly to warm within the same week. Layering is key during these months. Start with a light base layer, add a breathable middle layer, and have a wind-resistant jacket ready. Watch out for afternoon thunderstorms and plan your runs accordingly.

Summer: Heat Management

Summer running in Nebraska means early mornings or evening sessions. The key is to hydrate well, wear light-colored breathable clothing, and listen to your body. If the heat index is extreme, consider moving your run indoors or to an air-conditioned track. Acclimate gradually to warmer temperatures over several weeks.

Fall: Peak Running Season

Fall is arguably the best time to be a runner in Nebraska. Cool temperatures, beautiful foliage, and ideal conditions make this the perfect season for building fitness. Take advantage of the comfortable weather to increase mileage or work on pace. This is also prime time for fall marathons and half marathons.

Winter: Embrace the Cold

Winter running requires proper gear and mindset. The key layers include a moisture-wicking base, an insulating middle layer, and a wind-resistant outer shell. Don't forget a hat or headband for your ears and gloves that can be easily removed as you warm up. Reflective gear and lights are essential for those early morning or evening runs when it's still dark.

Trail Running Opportunities

Nebraska offers excellent trail running options throughout the year. From the Loess Hills in the west to the trails at Platte River State Park, there's no shortage of scenic routes to explore. Each season offers a different perspective on these beautiful landscapes.

Adapting Your Training

Be flexible with your training plan based on weather conditions. If a winter storm hits, it's okay to move your long run to another day or do a treadmill workout. Consistency over time matters more than any single workout. Use seasonal transitions as recovery periods and build your base for the next season.
